author: 305 Trader
content: Hey wakefieldI I am doing something which is very hard but it is my challenge. I am rebuilding a $1,500 account that went down this year gradually to $28 recently about 3 weeks ago. I said I will not add anymore and trade 1x con until I hit $200-$300. I am half way now. So I only trade 0DTE in QQQ using NVDA as my benchmark and not the way around. Its been working. Loss aversion is now under control. I use a mechanical stop loss but I place a limit order on it that I drag slightly up or down to my chosen price level as the options momentum goes up or down. But always a $15-20 stop loss. Until I move to 2x cons. Then it will become $25-30.

author: bluedog_sd#0001
content: I'm trying to manage my expectations on my risk/reward and it's confusing seeing Tony's results.   Today I what seemed like a very solid trade on TSLA 350 calls.  My fill was $3.39 at 9:36.   Tony entered (on text) after me and the best possible fill could have been maybe 2.85 but probably closer to 3.00.   He posted a screenshot when the cons were $5.50 showing 40k profit.  If he took 100 cons which is what I think he takes, I don't get it.    FWIW the peak price on those cons was $6.85 which happened later than that and it seems that 40k/100 cons means  $4 profit for con.   For me my trade wound up being a 1:1.5 risk to reward, which seems to be generally what happens on upside trades for me.   My risk was $1 on the con, the bottom line is I don't know what I'm doing wrong, but I'm also confused at how to get this type of result when the math doesn't seem to make sense to me.

author: Carve
content: They usually state the contract (Call/put and strike price) they are looking at and wait for the entry point (this could take some time).  If they see a good entry and take it, they would announce it, ie. "picked up 350 Tesla calls".  The actual contract price depends on the entry point - but this is typically not noted from what I have observed.

author: Alessandro
content:
Hi everyone!
Sorry, I hope I’m not being too long-winded…
I’m getting into options, and even though I went through the dedicated module, there’s still something I don’t fully understand.
In every video, Jack and Tony show a chart where a break & retest happens, followed by an R:R. For example, the one that just happened on Nvidia could have been a trade with an R:R of 1:9 — meaning with $100 risk I could’ve made $900. I don’t think I’ve misunderstood up to this point, but please correct me if I have…
That said, they apply the trade on options. I was looking at the puts, and the first OTM was the $170 strike (with the live price being just above $171 at that moment). I don’t remember the exact BID and ASK, but they were around $2.00 per contract, let’s round it for simplicity (so the premium to pay to enter the buy put was $200).
The trade on TradingView would’ve closed at $169.50, and at that point the BID/ASK was around $2.85 per contract. So, assuming I bought 1 contract, I’d have risked $200 to close the trade at $285. That’s an $85 profit (after commissions, maybe around $80 net).
Am I missing something here?
Where does the R:R from the videos come into play? Because this contract would’ve ended up with an R:R of just 1.4 
Thank you

author: Markellwhite16
content: Just a Reminder ❤️ trading is all probability not certainties . EVREY trade we take we don’t know if it’s gonna be a loser or a winner. Wins and losses doesn’t make you a good trader. What makes a great trader is someone who follow their rules , system , and stay discipline. You can’t judge your performance off PnL but based off those things ☝🏾. ALWAYS keep a long term mindset. Can’t focus on one day. Focus on results at the end of the week , month , quarter , and year. Always keep the long term mindset! If you lost today . Journal & move on and come in ready tomorrow with a fresh mindset and don’t brings today’s outcomes and emotions into tomorrow! 💪🏾

author: Jerry
content:
Hey guys,

Wanted to share my experience of going from struggling to profitable (just a few months so far, but still). Maybe it can be a good starting point for some of you—it really changed the game for me.
I struggled a lot before. At one point, I was trading 20 TSLA contracts, taking small wins but then wiping everything out with big losses. Ended up down almost $15k in just a few months. That’s when I stopped and decided to really think.
I built an Excel table with all of Tony’s trades this year, split by symbol. Every row was a trade with entry (I added some delay since I’m slower), stop loss, max potential win until SL was hit, etc. I kept adding parameters and I’m still revisiting them, improving execution.
From that analysis, I found the main thing that killed my profitability: scaling out too early. I used to sell 30% on the HOD pop, then more on the next big candle, and my RR was completely ruined.
So I made a script in Excel to simulate different scenarios and find the best monthly outcome. The result was simple: don’t scale at all until TSLA moves at least $4 (around 2R), then exit. That’s it.
That one change completely flipped my trading. Now I trade 10 contracts and make way more than I did before with 20. This is just the first step, of course. Next is working on dynamic sizing based on setup quality, scaling in/out properly, etc. But this shift was the key—it stopped the bleeding and got me back to making money.

Hope it will help

author: Jerry
content: Yeah exactly. I did the same analysis for amd, Nvidia and appl. Found the profit targets for amd and Nvidia, decided to completely avoid appl, cause the results there (win rate and RR) are quite bad cause good entry and scaling out is essential with this ticker

author: Angel (az)
content: Thanks so much for sharing this, that's super interesting and will be doing a similar analysis as soon as I get a chance! Been trying to finetune my own scaling and trying to figure out whether to change - in the past I typically sold one third at each TP, with TP being about $5 away on TSLA, really similar to what you mentioned, and those have been my best trades. But I was second-guessing my scaling lately because like you said, it's so difficult to lose all these trades where Tony/Jack are coming out green or breakeven, makes it feel like the losses are avoidable. And so many are scaling 60-70% at HOD (minus contextual) but I just don't see how that as a base scaling strategy can cover the losses over time and the RR just isn't working out on paper for me. Am super curious how Tony and others manage that, perhaps with the much sharper executions?

author: Jerry
content:
Yeah. Doing your own analysis is essential. Only that way you will get confidence. I simulated different appproaches.
1. No scaling. That gives the best results - best RR and 50% win rate
2. 1 scale. Again tried to find an optimum with simulation where and how many to scale to improve win rate at the price of RR. Managed to do it. Increased to 65% win rate with some combinations but it hit the total profit.
3. 1 scale + runners. Same results as in second approach.

So after all simulations no scaling approach gives the best profit, though much more complicated from mental perspective. 

To the question why Tony manages to have such great result - i see 3 points
1. Much better entry so good RR on HOD
2. Scaling based on good reading of price action like scaling out when several wicks as a sign of weakness
3. Scaling out base on context and not on predefined structure.

But all these points come with experience. We will get there someday

author: G Nice
content: Hey guys, for small accounts (1-2k) or those just starting out. I would highly recommend trading shares instead of options until you have a solid setup/trading plan established. You will have far less anxiety trading shares as it doesn’t move as fast as options (a $1 drop in share price will be a $1 P&L loss/share. where an ATM options contract is typically around $50 loss/con). You can still trade the B&R live with the group and practice your entry, scales and SL as if they are options except it will not blow your account. Do this until you are consistently profitable and have a solid trading plan. The point is not the amount of money you make in shares but mainly to establish a solid scalable trading plan that you can transfer over to options when you are ready. I hate to see/hear people blowing their accounts or losing more than they can manage so I hope this helps.

author: Angel (az)
content:
Thanks for the details - when I was backtesting by myself quite a while back, I was always just around breakeven and I thought it was because I wasn’t identifying the setups correctly but I’ve realized over time it’s most likely this scaling issue, because my win rate tends to match. So scaling is really my last (and big) missing piece. It’s such a good idea to do this scaling analysis on their trades first to eliminate any concerns and variables about my own skill issues in identifying the setup though. 

I think you hit the nail on the head with how Tony can manage the large HOD scales. I’ve been suspecting too that the thing we’re missing is execution speed for better RR, discretion, context and experience. Which are very, very difficult things to quantify and aim to improve other than putting in the time and being patient and then having a bit of faith in our brains to figure it out. So this was a useful reminder that we simply might not be currently ready to scale the same way. 

Thanks for all that, this exact problem has been at the back of my head and bothering me all week and this helped me clarify a lot!
